topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic rhythm I couldn't slow down. I clicked it open, my fingers trembling so badly I nearly missed the link. The headline was mine—my meticulously crafted piece on urban gardening—but the byline beneath it belonged to a complete stranger. A name I didn't recognize. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward. I reread the text, searching for any misplaced word, any sign of tampering. Every sentence felt alien, a violation. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my apartment, the floorboards groaning under my hurried steps. My breath hitched in my throat. Who had done this? I felt a dizzying sense of exposure, as if every private thought had been laid bare for public consumption by the wrong person.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my vision blurring slightly. The realization hit me with the force of a physical impact. My article, the one I poured months of late nights and careful research into, was published under someone else's name. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my hands slick with sweat. I backed away from the computer, bumping against the wall. My chest felt tight, constricted, as if something heavy was pressing down on my lungs. I kept glancing over my shoulder, half expecting someone to be lurking in the doorway. A shaky exhale escaped my lips. This wasn't just a mistake; this felt like a deliberate act of theft, a complete hijacking of my voice. I wanted to scream, but only a dry, rasping sound emerged.

[story 3]

When I finally read the confirmation, the color drained from my face. It was impossible. My contribution, my unique perspective, was being presented as another's achievement. A profound tremor ran through my hands, making the keyboard feel slippery and useless. I sank into my chair, pulling my knees up to my chest, trying to make myself small. The air in the room seemed too thick to breathe. Every small sound—the hum of the refrigerator, the distant traffic—seemed amplified, pressing in on me. I kept wiping my palms on my jeans, the friction a desperate attempt to ground myself. A deep, unsettling certainty settled in my gut: I needed to figure out what to do, but for now, all I could do was sit there, utterly immobilized by the sheer wrongness of the situation.
